1: wĕʾēlleh ʾašer niḥălû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan ʾašer niḥălû ʾōtām ʾelʿāzār hakōhēn wîhôšuaʿ bin-nûn wĕrāʾšê hāʾăbôt — 'these are what the Israelites received as inheritance in the land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the fathers' [houses] distributed to them.' Three-party oversight: priest (covenant mediator), commander (covenantal executor), and tribal elders (community representatives). The process is ecclesiastical, military, and civic simultaneously — no single party controls the distribution.
2: bĕgôrāl naḥălātām kāʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH bĕyad-mōšeh — 'by lot was their inheritance, as YHWH commanded through Moses.' The gôrāl (lot) is the divine decision-mechanism that removes human favoritism from the distribution. Proverbs 16:33 theologically grounds this: 'The lot is cast into the lap, but every decision is from YHWH.' The lot is not a random procedure but an oracular one — YHWH assigns each tribe its place.
3: Moses had already given the two and a half trans-Jordan tribes their portions; Levi receives no land. The structured recapitulation (Levi noted here and in v.4b) is not redundancy but legal clarity: the nine and a half tribes' lots are about to begin; the exceptions are established first so the reader knows the scope of what follows.
4: kî-hāyû bĕnê yôsēp šĕnê maṭṭôt mĕnaššeh wĕʾeprayim — 'for the sons of Joseph were two tribes, Manasseh and Ephraim.' The Joseph division is the mechanism by which twelve tribal lots fill twelve land portions despite Levi's exclusion. Jacob's deathbed elevation of Ephraim and Manasseh to tribal status (Gen 48:5: 'they shall be mine... as Reuben and Simeon they shall be mine') is the legal basis for this arithmetic. The Levitical cities and pasture lands (v.4b) are distinct from tribal allotments — Levi's distributed presence throughout the land embodies the nation's priestly calling.
5: kaʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet-mōšeh kēn ʿāśû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyaḥlĕqû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — 'as YHWH commanded Moses, so the Israelites did, and they divided the land.' The obedience formula closes the introduction and opens the Caleb narrative. The allotment process is an act of covenant obedience, not administrative convenience.
6: wayyiggĕšû bĕnê yĕhûdāh ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ bĕgilgāl wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw kālēb ben-yĕpunneh haqqĕnizzî — 'the men of Judah approached Joshua at Gilgal, and Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite said to him.' Caleb the Kenizzite is a non-Israelite by ancestry (the Kenizzites are listed in Gen 15:19 among the peoples of the land) who is fully incorporated into Judah. His access to the allotment as a full heir demonstrates that covenant membership — not ethnic lineage — determines inheritance rights. The appeal to 'what YHWH said to Moses' (ʾet-ʾašer dibber YHWH ʾel-mōšeh) grounds his claim in prophetic promise, not merely military service.
7: ben-ʾarbaʿîm šānāh ʾānōkî bišloaḥ mōšeh ʿebed YHWH ʾōtî — 'I was forty years old when Moses the servant of the LORD sent me.' The age specificity (forty at the reconnaissance, eighty-five now, v.10) structures the narrative: forty-five years from promise to fulfilment. Caleb's report: kaʾašer ʿim-lĕbābî — 'just as it was in my heart,' meaning the report he gave was his genuine conviction, not diplomatic accommodation. The ʾăšer ʿim-lĕbābî phrase connects sincerity of report with sincerity of faith.
8: wĕʾaḥay ʾašer ʿālû ʿimmî himmasîû ʾet-lēb hāʿām wĕʾānōkî millēʾtî ʾaḥărê YHWH ʾĕlōhāy — 'my fellow scouts made the heart of the people melt, but I fully followed after YHWH my God.' The key verb: millēʾtî from mālaʾ (be full, fill) + ʾaḥărê (after, behind). Millēʾ ʾaḥărê YHWH is the book's distinctive idiom for wholehearted covenantal loyalty — used of Caleb in Num 14:24; 32:12; Deut 1:36; here and in vv.9,14. The idiom images following as 'filling the space behind' YHWH — not reluctant compliance but total engagement of will.
9: wayyiššābaʿ mōšeh bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr ʾim-lōʾ hāʾāreṣ ʾašer dārakāh raglĕkā bāh lĕkā tihyeh lĕnaḥălāh — 'Moses swore on that day: the land where your feet have walked shall be your inheritance.' The oath grounds Caleb's claim in prophetic promise (Num 14:24; Deut 1:36) ratified by Mosaic oath. Forty-five years of waiting has not diminished the claim; the oath remains binding because it was sworn before YHWH.
10: wĕʿattāh hinnēh heḥěyāh YHWH ʾōtî kaʾašer dibber — 'now, look — YHWH has kept me alive just as he promised.' Heḥěyāh (kept alive, from ḥāyāh) is the same verb used of YHWH preserving life through the wilderness; Caleb attributes his forty-five-year survival not to constitution but to divine faithfulness. mizzeh ʾarbaʿîm wĕḥāmēš šānāh — forty-five years since the Sinai promise, twenty-five of which were conquest campaigns. The arithmetic of grace: the promise was made; the time has passed; YHWH has delivered.
11: kōḥî ʿōdennû hayyôm kaʾašer kōḥî bĕyôm šĕloaḥ mōšeh ʾōtî — 'my strength today is still as my strength was on the day Moses sent me.' The claim is remarkable: eighty-five years old, and Caleb asserts physical parity with his forty-year-old self. Whether read literally (YHWH's supernatural preservation) or rhetorically (relative vigor, not comparative measurement), the statement is a theology of divine sustenance — Caleb's vitality is evidence of YHWH's faithfulness to his promise.
12: wĕʿattāh tĕnāh-lî ʾet-hāhār hazzeh — 'now give me this hill country!' The imperative is bold — Caleb is not requesting but claiming. Hāhār hazzeh ('this hill country') is Hebron and its Anakite-held heights. Caleb had seen the Anakites and was unafraid at Kadesh (Num 13:33); now at eighty-five he asks for the hardest territory, the place where the giants live. ûlay YHWH ʾittî wĕhôraštîm kaʾašer dibber YHWH — 'perhaps YHWH will be with me, and I shall drive them out just as YHWH has said.' The ûlay ('perhaps, maybe') is not doubt but covenantal humility — Caleb frames the conquest as contingent on divine presence, not guaranteed by his vigor.
13: waybārekhēhû yĕhôšuaʿ wayyittēn ʾet-ḥebrôn lĕkālēb ben-yĕpunneh lĕnaḥălāh — 'Joshua blessed him and gave Hebron to Caleb son of Jephunneh as inheritance.' The priestly blessing (bārak) before the grant marks this as a covenant act, not merely an administrative transfer. Hebron — Kiriath-arba, the city of Arba the greatest of the Anakites — is given to the man who was fearless before Anakites forty-five years earlier. Promise and fulfillment are calibrated: the specific fear that disqualified the first generation is overcome by the specific faith of the one who refused to fear.
14: ʿal-kēn hāyĕtāh ḥebrôn lĕkālēb ben-yĕpunneh haqqĕnizzî lĕnaḥălāh ʿad hayyôm hazzeh yaʿan ʾašer millēʾ ʾaḥărê YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'therefore Hebron has belonged to Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite as inheritance to this day, because he wholeheartedly followed YHWH God of Israel.' The causal chain is explicit: the inheritance is the consequence of wholehearted following. Millēʾ ʾaḥărê YHWH (appearing for the final time) is the book's summary verdict on Caleb's life: one who filled the space behind YHWH completely. The 'to this day' formula anchors the etiological claim — Hebron as Calebite territory is a historical fact requiring explanation, and the explanation is theological.
15: wĕšem ḥebrôn lĕpānîm qiryat-ʾarbaʿ — 'the former name of Hebron was Kiriath-arba.' The rename from 'city of Arba' (the greatest Anakite) to 'Hebron' (from ḥābar, 'friend/confederate') may reflect Hebrew renaming practice after conquest. Arba's name and greatness are preserved even in the record of his people's defeat — the text does not erase the enemy's memory but notes it within the victory account. Wattišqōt hāʾāreṣ mimmilḥāmāh — 'and the land had rest from war.' The šāqaṭ (rest/quiet) formula closes this section as it closed ch.11:23; each major phase of the narrative ends with land-rest as the theological verdict: YHWH's wars, when completed, produce peace.
